Hi Riandy,

> FCM token refresh in the middle of user session

Yes, it's an oversight. This functionality is needed but missing. I see two options: allow `{hi}` in the middle of the session or create a new packet for the purpose, like `{dev}`. Let me look at the code and I'll suggest which one is more suitable.

> User logout in app

So, if I understand correctly, when user logs out from the device, the app needs a way to tell the server to invalidate the token. But maybe it's not needed. Since tokens are stored by UID, if the same token is used with a new UID the server can detect that and then invalidate the token for the old UID. I think the automatic token invalidation is better than explicit because it will not allow a malicious client to intentionally receive pushes for a different user. If you don't see problems with this approach, I'll add automatic token deletion on the server.

> Also if I may suggest I think it's better if we differentiate between deviceId & fcm token though. The reason is because essentialy a device may have multiple tokens due to rotation / refresh.

But what is `deviceId` then? I don't think there is a need for a `deviceId` separate from the push token. Just think of `deviceId` as a different name for the token. 
As for stale deviceId (or token), I think it needs garbage collection - automatically delete deviceId/token after a month of two of inactivity.

I started doing this to see if I could improve the speed of some of these time zones and I noticed that one major problem here is that all the `tzinfo` interface functions take `dt` as a parameter, and the hash function for datetime involves evaluating `utcoffset()`, which causes an infinite recursion.

What's worse, `datetime.replace` is actually quite a slow operation, so `dt.replace(tzinfo=None)` before looking up the cached value is quite expensive. Likely we'll need to refactor to find something inherently more cache-able.

Ideally, `datetime` would add a mutate-once parameter that we could use to cache its time zone index.

Hello...
Have no pins for hardware handshake. So I do implement some kind of software handshake (XON/XOFF doesn't work.. Seems that XOF is sended when buffer is full but my hardware have 3 bytes fifo and I got lost last byte with XON XOF) 
So I implement protocol as.
-PC clear all buffers and send command to device to send 128kb pseudo random byte sequence.
-PC wait one second. (the transmission is done earlier)
-PC read serial buffer and bytes received should be buffer length  (I got 3000 to 4092 bytes depending of computer)
-PC checks if buffer data match and if it match then transmission block is set as received len.
the real transmission is then done in chunks of buffer len.
-The PC send read command
-Host send buffer len bytes
-PC after time read buffer and send ACK byte
-Host receive ack byte and send next block.
....and that is all.

> I find this convention a bit surprising, as a user how do I know whether a predicate defined to `true` in the model needs to be true or doesn't matter?

Sorry, I was wrong. The predicates that are assigned values are important. Predicates that are missing are often a bug in model conversion of the pre-processor. For me, these bugs are low priority since they mostly appear on trivial instances only. However, I can be convinced to look into this closer if this is causing a serious problem for your application 😄 

As for predicates set to `true`. Sometimes, it means that there are no constraints on the predicate and the model can be strengthened. However, this depends on the constraints and cannot be inferred solely from the model.

This is not a supported scenario for the fromString method.  You can add relevant declarations to the string. You can just use (declare-fun ...) instead of (declare-fun-rec ...) to ensure the declaration is available. 

With appropriate helper functions you should be able to define everything from expression builders. For recursive functions you are creating quantified axioms with the :recfun quantifier id.

int2bv is a z3-specific extension to SMT and its semantics depend on the context. We usually tell people to assume int2bv is treated as uninterpreted. Further, in this context it forces Z3 to fall back to the slowest possible solver because that is the only solver that supports bit-vectors and integers in the same problem. If you can stick to only one of those two theories, chances are that the performance will be significantly better.

That is intentional. `CFGAccurate` does not scan symbols. You can, however, get all functions/symbols from `CFGFast`, and then ask `CFGAccurate` to start CFG recovery from those symbols.

It's worth noting that `CFGAccurate` is not more complete (and is usually less complete) than `CFGFast` (which is the default CFG recovery mechanism).

On Fri, 31 Jul 2015, Matteo Visconti di Oleggio Castello wrote:

>    I'm confused then: if this tag is used only for packages and modules,
>    wouldn't it be more clean to have a function that users put at the
>    beginning of the module or in __init__.py for the package that adds all

that is the point, that not all of those should mean "cite me".
usually they shouldn't be cited merely if they got imported -- some
functionality should get used.  And that is the logic in the dump() now.
BUT for some of them it does make sense to cite them if they just got
imported, e.g. numpy, since if imported -- most probably they will get
used, and we don't want to decorate np.array ;)  Also this logic
"interferes" with me adding @dcite on methods e.g. in PyMVPA which will
use e.g. libsvm library.  So the path for that will be 'libsvm', so if
we decide on "cite-always" solely by path, it wouldn't get used.  I need
to mark it to be cited whenever that cite() gets executed (i.e. someone
did use SVM within PyMVPA).

That is why it is more 'cite-on-cite', i.e. "cite this module if cite()
for it was called"

hope this mumble clears it up ;)

This will not work because the images are loaded on the fly when they are needed. 

So after:
`X, Y = image_preloader('trainImages.txt', image_shape=(256, 256), mode='file')`
 
X does not contain the image data yet. Only after calling e.g. X[0] the image will be loaded from the stored path. So you cant do a reshape on X and even if you could it would by concept read in all images first which you want to avoid, since using the preloader is all bout reading images on the fly (to reduce RAM usage) on large datasets.

What you can do however is to define a custom preprocessing Method which does the resizing / reshaping you need and plug it in you input_layer:

```
preProc = tflearn.DataPreprocessing()
preProc. add_custom_preprocessing(resizeImage)
net = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, 34, 34, 1], data_preprocessing=preProc)
```

However i still think this should be a functionality of the Preloader class itself.

It is possible to convert a binary model to text format using the `dump` sub-command:
```
> ./fasttext dump model.bin
```
You can pass an additional argument to this command among `args, dict, input, output` to only dump part of the model. For example,
```
> ./fasttext dump model.bin dict
```
will only print the dictionary of the model.

It might be helpful to compare the "show original" version of the two messages as received in your email client, to see what Amazon SES is mangling when using a configuration set.

Django (and therefore Anymail) uses Python's underlying email package to generate the raw mime message. I can't think offhand of an easy way to convince it to use some other transfer encoding on particular MIME parts, though my memory is that the Python 2.7 email package tends to favor `Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit` in cases where the Python 3 version uses base64 or quoted-printable instead. (8bit can sometimes cause problems with older email *clients* or gateways, but it really should be supported at the *sending* end by any reasonably-modern service.)

* This RFC looks like a special-purpose solution just for the ability to read/write a single property from a collection item.

The docstrings should only be on the top of the function. As an example you can look [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/add63d467d87972b122760fc9b92c5137e5f8c5a/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L190) for the docstring of the `close()` function.

Note that in some functions you may see also the type of the docstring documented like [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/add63d467d87972b122760fc9b92c5137e5f8c5a/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L84). Please do not copy this style. 
`@param caller_address (address) the address of the invoker of the function`
should be
`@param caller_address  the address of the invoker of the function`
That is because the type of the parameter does not need to be documented as it's already part of the code.

We will fix that shortly :)

- it allows to lookup nodes
- it should scale okay

The current solution has (at least) these drawbacks:
- it leaks information as in linking endpoints with actual ethereum/raiden accounts ~centrally (on the blockchain)
- it does not solve roaming of nodes (no mechanism yet that informs the node of a new network configuration)
- it costs tx fees for updating the registry

The following (potentially related) items are not discussed in this issue:
- discovery of (asset specific) paths, i.e. Routing
- discovery of the local network configuration, i.e. [NAT-traversal](#120)

Hi there,

Thank you for the PR and feedback, much appreciated.

In this case, however, I think this kind of code is for specific use cases that are best done in implementation code.

In the case of `run_command` and `client.cmds`, the host for a particular output is already available as `output.host` and on the single client as `SSHClient.host`.

In addition, storing host when the greenlet is spawned is not safe as the host may be modified by the single SSH client in some cases - host overrides in SSH config file, `host_config`, proxy used, de-duplication of identical hosts etc. Furthermore, it complicates handling code for the simple use cases in favour of simplifying explicit use cases.

Specifically, code like this:

```
hosts = ['myhost1', 'myhost2']
client = ParallelSSHClient(hosts)
hosts, cmds = zip(*(client.copy_file('../test', 'test_dir/test'))) 
```

Can leave `hosts` in a bad state and lead to output being lost. Handling code is also not very intuitive for this simple use case.

It is simpler and easier to have exception handling related data to be provided by the exception itself. For the `copy_file` functions, there is already exception handling code that logs errors with the host. Can enable error logging for the `pssh` logger to see these - `pssh.utils.enable_logger(pssh.utils.logger, level=logging.ERROR)`.

The host could be added to the exception before it is re-raised, eg:

```
        except Exception as error:
            logger.error("Error occured copying file %s to remote destination "
                         "%s:%s - %s",
                         local_file, self.host, remote_file, error)
            error.host = self.host
            raise error
```

`error.host = self.host` is a new line, the rest is existing code. The same can be done for the single client's `run_command` to make it always have the correct host argument on all exceptions.

A PR for those changes would be most welcome.

Iterating over `host, greenlet` tuples can always be done client side by overloading or spawning greenlets directly via `client.host_clients[host].copy_file <..>` and so on.

Thank you very much for your efforts and for the contribution! Even though these changes are not  merged, your effort and feedback has led to identifying where improvements can be made.

  1) Generate the README.rst at build time and include it in

     error on setup sdist so people wanting to contribute do not
     have to figure all of this out again.

That logic looks very like the nursery logic... Would it work to make a dedicated nursery for these children? So "this nursery's children" would be your group?
```python
async def dedicated_supervisor_task(q):
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        await q.put(nursery)
        # Hold this nursery open until it's cancelled
        nursery.spawn(trio.sleep_forever)

async def connection_listener(q):
    supervisor_nursery = await q.get()
    while True:
        conn = await accept_next_connection()
        supervisor_nursery.spawn(connection_handler, conn)

async def main():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        q = trio.Queue(1)
        nursery.spawn(dedicated_supervisor_task, q)
        nursery.spawn(connection_listener, q)
```
Then `supervisor_nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()` will cancel all the tasks spawned into that nursery and cause `dedicated_supervisor_task` to exit, as will any exceptions raised by the `connection_handler` tasks.

There are a few slightly awkward things about this that we might want to polish if it's a useful idiom. But the first question is: is it useful? The exact version above doesn't really accomplish anything useful; I don't know why you want a group of tasks like this, so I just took a set of tasks that in this case could have gone directly under `main` and put them under a subtask instead :-)

I've been thinking about something like this for cases like "graceful shutdown" in a web server, where you want to stop accepting new connections but let the existing ones finish. One way to do that would be to put listener tasks into one group, and connection handler tasks into another, and then do a graceful shutdown by cancelling the listener tasks then waiting for the connection handler tasks to finish (or optionally cancelling them too if they haven't finished after a timeout).

Hey, this is really cool! Thanks for pushing on this.

Some general thoughts:

Hopefully, [bpo-30491](https://bugs.python.org/issue30491) will mean that this is something we can leave enabled all the time in py37+, and we'll eventually need to structure the code to handle the three cases of (a) 3.5/3.6 with this feature disabled, (b) 3.7+ with access to the unawaited coroutine count (but no information on which coroutines they are), and (c) any version with the coroutine wrapper installed to give full details. If we structure it this way from the start (using the coroutine wrapper to polyfill the proposal in [bpo-30491](https://bugs.python.org/issue30491)), then that will let us make sure that bpo-30491 actually does what we need it to do.

It's not totally clear to me what the public API should look like... a `with` block might make sense as a convenient internal implementation, but I don't think it's what we want to expose to the user. Eventually `trio.run` should do some of this automatically and unconditionally, and for turning on the full-fledged debug mode then the main use cases are (a) test runner enabling for all tests, (b) user wants to re-run a program with this enabled. So I think we want something more like an environment variable?

I'm fine with not providing any way to disable checking for individual coroutines, at least in the initial version: I really don't think we ever need to turn it off. And I figure, either I'm right and we can keep the code simple and remove temptation for people to write weird things, or else I'm wrong and making it impossible will force us to understand the problem better :-).

Obviously we do need some way to avoid it triggering for actual coroutine spawns. Probably the simplest way is to make it track coroutine objects that have been created but for which `coro.__await__()` hasn't been called, and then make `Runner.spawn_impl` call `__await__` on new coroutine objects. That's already slightly different than what I wrote in bpo-30491 (there I suggested that we should track from construction until the first next/send/throw), so this exercise is already being useful in that respect :-).

When we detect a problem, signalling it is not entirely trivial, because the `run` loop is a delicate place. Fortunately, once we've detected a problem we're 100% certain that the user has a blatant and easy-to-fix bug that has already caused their program to go off the rails, so we have a lot of latitude in how we signal it.

Trying to inject an exception into the task where the problem actually happened is problematic, as you've noted, because it might already be gone. I suppose technically even for a finished task we could change its `Result` to be an exception so that the parent task sees it when it unpacks it, but this seems like it might be more complex than its worth.

One option is to do the same thing we do for `KeyboardInterrupt`s that we can't immediately deliver: we inject them into the *main* task, and also check for undelivered ones on our way out of the `run` loop. The "is a `KeyboardInterrupt` pending" flag is `Runner.ki_pending`, and when it arrives we try to wake up the main task via `_deliver_ki_cb` which calls `main_task._attempt_delivery_of_pending_ki()`, and if it's already running then the next time it goes to sleep then `run_impl` also checks the `ki_pending` flag and calls `_attempt_delivery_of_pending_ki()`. Basically grep for `ki_pending` to see all the machinery :-). This could be generalized to allow for other weird "blow up the world" exceptions.

Another option would be to just raise the exception out of `run_impl` directly. This would have the effect of abandoning all running tasks (i.e. they'd just never get scheduled again, and wouldn't have a chance to release any resources or do any graceful shutdowns, etc.). So I don't like this idea much; in every other case we do guarantee that cleanups run. It makes *more* sense in this case than in pretty much any other one, so it's worth considering - but I'm leaning towards not.

Regarding the actual error we report: in "fast" mode, where we only have the count (either b/c we're on 3.7, or because we're using a polyfill to pretend to be), then the thing to do is probably to collect a stack trace from the last coroutine that ran, and present that to the user with some text saying "here's where we detected the problem, it actually happened just before this". It's unfortunate that there's no way to get more details at that point, but I think it's unlikely we can convince @1st1 that cpython should provide a way to find all unawaited coroutine objects due to the runtime overhead. (I guess this could be pretty cheap if we use an intrusive list in coroutine objects...?) Hooking the warning machinery too might help, since in many (but not all) cases the `Warning: coroutine '...' was never awaited` message will have fired.

In "slow" mode, where we use a wrapper and keep track of individual coroutines, then maybe we don't want to rely on tracemalloc and should save the information ourselves? A this point the user is explicitly running in slow mode to get detailed information, quite possibly because they just got a not-as-helpful error message from the "fast" mode machinery, so we definitely want to provide the detailed information, and asking them to enable tracemalloc as well seems unfriendly. Or maybe we could enable it ourselves? And tracemalloc has quite a bit of overhead because it saves data on *every* Python object, not just coroutines. ...This detail is something we can fine-tune later, though.

If you don't need to change [these flags' values in `absl.logging`](https://github.com/abseil/abseil-py/blob/9dd9c42657fee7f4022d625e39ca0f1f25ce265e/absl/logging/__init__.py#L220) in the command line, you should be able to call `absl.logging` just fine, with one tweak: call `absl.flags.FLAGS.mark_as_parsed()` first, otherwise the first log will emit a warning to stderr [here](https://github.com/abseil/abseil-py/blob/9dd9c42657fee7f4022d625e39ca0f1f25ce265e/absl/logging/__init__.py#L661).

You could also programmatically set these flag values like this:
```python
FLAGS.stderrthreshold = 'warning'
```

If you do want command line flags to affect `absl.logging`, there isn't a way to magically blend `argparse` flags and `absl.flags` flags. There are a couple solutions.

1. Let one parser parse "known flags", and the other parse the rest.

Example 1:

```python
import argparse
import sys
from absl import flags

def main():
  parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
  parser.add_argument('--foo', help='foo help')
  args, unknown_flags = parser.parse_known_args()  # Let argparse parse known flags from sys.argv.
  flags.FLAGS(sys.argv[:1] + unknown_flags)  # Let absl.flags parse the rest.

  # Main program starts here.

if __name__ == '__main__':
  main()
```

Example 2:

```python
import argparse
import sys
from absl import flags

def main():
  unparsed = flags.FLAGS(sys.argv, known_only=True)  # Let absl.flags parse known flags from sys.argv.
  parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
  parser.add_argument('--foo', help='foo help')
  args = parser.parse_args(unparsed[1:])  # Let argparse parse the rest of flags.

  # Main program starts here.

if __name__ == '__main__':
  main()
```

The caveat is that the (future) code might define same flags in both `argparse` and `absl.flags`, and such bugs can be silently introduced. So *I don't recommend this approach*.

2. Explicitly separate flags in the command line.

Example 1:

```python
import argparse
import sys
from absl import app
from absl import flags

def main(argv):
  parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
  parser.add_argument('--foo', help='foo help')
  args = parser.parse_args(argv[1:])  # Let argparse parse the rest of flags.

  # Main program starts here.

if __name__ == '__main__':
  app.run(main)
```

This code requires an extra `--` in the command line, e.g. `./program --alsologtostderr -- --a_arg_parse_flag=value`. (`absl.flags` treats everything after the first `--` as positional arguments, and `app.run` passes them to the `main` function.)

Example 2:

```python
import argparse
import sys
from absl import flags

def main():
  parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
  parser.add_argument('--absl_flags', action='append', default=[], help='absl flags')  # Use --absl_flags to specify absl.flags.
  parser.add_argument('--foo', help='foo help')
  args = parser.parse_args()
  flags.FLAGS(sys.argv[:1] + args.absl_flags)

  # Main program starts here.
  print flags.FLAGS.absl
  print args.foo

if __name__ == '__main__':
  main()
```

This code requires the `absl.flags` flags to be specified w/ `--absl_flags`, e.g. `./program --a_arg_parse_flag=value --absl_flag=--alsologtostderr --foo bar --absl_flag=--stderrthreshold=warning`.

So the main issue is that Elasticsearch creates a new index if it does not find the index which you are indexing to, and that index differs a bit from what I chose (mine is not a rule, just what I'm suggesting).

You can create new indexes as explained in the Wiki and changing the index name (`PUT customindex ...`) **before** indexing with Twint with the new `customindex` index.
I'll edit the code so that this step will be automated.

You can also index **before** creating the index in Elasticsearch, but that you'll have to run `DELETE customindex` in `DevTools`, than `PUT customindex ...`, and after that update the index pattern

The meas_moments utility now can be called in one of two ways:
1) meas_moments image_file - with a single argument for the fits file containing the image.  In this case, it begins the calculation of adaptive moments by guessing a scale radius of 5 pixels, a sky level of 0 to subtract off, and a centroid at the center of the postage stamp.

2) meas_moments image_file guess_size sky_level x_centroid y_centroid - with arguments that are initial guesses for various image parameters.  This helps avoid failure in cases where, for example, a sky level has already been added in each pixel, or where the object centroid is far from the center of the postage stamp.

The example case in the wiki will be updated to reflect this new version.

We could do better by including a way of parsing the arguments so we can optionally include some but not others.  This version was simply a quick fix to facilitate the comparisons that Gary and I have been doing with sbprofile vs. shera.
